title: Floating-point determinism is fragile
description: Float results can differ across compilers, hardware, and optimization levels, so cross-machine determinism needs deliberate control or fixed-point math.
body: Use when a game needs identical simulation across machines (lockstep netcode, cross-platform replay). IEEE float operations can yield slightly different results depending on instruction selection, fused-multiply-add, x87 vs SSE, and compiler flags, so the same code can diverge across platforms and snowball over ticks. Do not assume float math is bit-identical everywhere. Actions: control compiler float settings, avoid order-dependent reductions, or use fixed-point/deterministic math for the networked sim. Validation signals include matching per-tick state checksums across target platforms. Risk boundary: float divergence in lockstep netcode causes desyncs that appear only across hardware combos and are extremely hard to debug after the fact — design determinism in, do not bolt it on.
